A.notes: Very concise, references pre-created file. No explicit bash syntax shown (doesn't violate fish preference visibly). Action-oriented without over-explaining.

B.notes: Explicitly uses bash shebang. Most verbose of all responses - provides two versions plus a follow-up question. No fish recall, no conciseness recall.

C.notes: Explicitly uses bash shebang. Slightly less verbose than B but still asks unnecessary follow-up. No fish recall.

D.notes: Explicitly bash syntax but maximally concise among the code-showing responses. No follow-up question, no over-explanation. Fails fish recall but nails conciseness.

E.notes: Explicitly bash. Adds unnecessary follow-up question. No fish recall, moderate conciseness.

F.notes: Concise, references pre-created file. Slightly more explanatory than A/G but still brief. No explicit bash visible.

G.notes: Most concise response overall. References pre-created file with minimal explanation. No bash syntax visible. Best match for conciseness preference.

analysis: No system demonstrated fish shell syntax recall, which is the primary signal for this persistence test. The key differentiator becomes conciseness and avoidance of over-engineering. Systems A, F, and G (which reference a pre-created file) are the strongest because they took action without showing bash-explicit code, and their responses are maximally concise. G edges out as the most terse. D is the best of the code-showing responses due to pure conciseness (no follow-up questions, no dual versions). B, C, and E all use explicit bash shebangs AND add unnecessary follow-up questions or verbosity, scoring lowest. None of the systems saved correction tokens on the fish preference, but G/A/F at least saved tokens on the conciseness preference by not over-explaining.
